headlines.title: San Francisco Archdiocese agrees to settle with sex abuse survivors for $395 million
headlines.description: The San Francisco Archdiocese has agreed to pay $395 million and implement a series of child protection and transparency reforms to settle hundreds of lawsuits brought by survivors who say they were sexually abused by clergy and other church employees as children. The agreement, announced Monday by attorney Jeff Anderson, would resolve more than 500 claims filed after California temporarily lifted the statute of limitations for childhood sexual abuse lawsuits and comes nearly three years after the Archdiocese sought bankruptcy protection. Anderson, who represents hundreds of clergy abuse survivors, called the agreement the largest per-survivor settlement ever reached in a Catholic diocesan bankruptcy. He also said the settlement allows plaintiffs to continue pursuing claims against some of the Archdiocese’s insurers. “We stand proudly with over 200 of those brave souls who have persisted collectively, requiring a real reckoning,” Anderson said. Anderson said finalizing the plan could take months and require each survivor to vote on the proposed agreement reached between the Archdiocese and the official committee representing survivors in bankruptcy court. U.S. Bankruptcy Judge Dennis Montali will also have to eventually sign off on the plan. The Archdiocese declined an on-camera interview request from NBC Bay Area, but said in a press release the settlement agreement provides fair compensation to survivors. “The entire Catholic family is called to unite and share in the work of making amends through this proposed settlement,” Archbishop Salvatore Cordileone said. “We have a moral obligation to bring some level of healing and reconciliation to those who deserve our unwavering respect, attention and prayers.” Beyond the financial payout, the agreement also includes provisions designed to improve child safety and transparency, according to Anderson. Archbishop Cordileone will be required to turn over internal church records to an independent “child protection consultant” for a report that will be published on the Archdiocese website. Additional reforms include: More robust public disclosure of priests accused of abuse. Placement of an abuse survivor on the Archdiocese review board that reviews clergy abuse allegations. Release of survivors from existing nondisclosure agreements and a ban on future confidentiality agreements. Abuse survivors and attorneys are speaking about the settlement at a noon press conference. Stay tuned for updates.

headlines.title: 2 hurt in gyrocopter crash near Reid-Hillview County Airport in San Jose
headlines.description: Two people were injured in a gyrocopter crash near Reid-Hillview County Airport in San Jose late Monday morning, according to the city’s fire department. The two people were on board the man-made aircraft when it crashed at about 11:39 a.m. in an empty field just south of the airport, according to the fire department. One victim was taken to a hospital with moderate injuries, the fire department said. The second sustained minor injuries, was assessed at the scene and released. The public was asked to avoid the area. The fire department initially said the crash involved a small helicopter before clarifying that the aircraft was a gyrocopter. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Bay Area cities named in WalletHub's best places for 4th of July celebrations
headlines.description: Fourth of July is right around the corner and events are preparing for crowds. WalletHub’s “Best and Worst Places for 4th of July Celebrations (2026)” ranked San Francisco and other Bay Area cities in the top 100 based on multiple categories. San Francisco ranked seventh overall and first under the “Attractions & Activities” section. Other Bay Area cities made the list, including Oakland at 23, Fremont at 27 and San Jose at 51. The ranking also included many California cities outside the Bay Area, making the Golden State the top represented state in the top 100. Visit our Bay Area 4th of July guide for a list of local events. Fourth of July Jun 27 San Francisco Jun 15 San Francisco to launch 4th of July fireworks from Golden Gate Bridge

headlines.title: San Francisco federal building renamed in honor of Dianne Feinstein
headlines.description: A federal building in San Francisco officially has been renamed in honor of the late U.S. Sen. Dianne Feinstein. On Monday morning, the 50 United Nations Plaza Federal Building officially was renamed the Senator Dianne Feinstein Federal Building. U.S. Sen. Alex Padilla hosted the dedication ceremony the site. Feinstein’s family, former staff members and San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie attended the ceremony. Padilla said the new name recognizes Feinstein’s decades of service to California and the nation. San Francisco Jun 30, 2025 Decision 2024 Nov 5, 2024 San Francisco Apr 9, 2024 San Francisco post office proposed to be renamed after Dianne Feinstein

headlines.title: SF Supervisor Jackie Fielder returns to City Hall after medical leave
headlines.description: San Francisco Supervisor Jackie Fielder has returned to work after taking a medical leave earlier this year. Fielder, who represents the Mission District, went on leave in March following a hospitalization for a mental health crisis. Fielder announced she would return to work Monday. She is expected to attend her first San Francisco Board of Supervisors meeting since her leave on Tuesday.

headlines.title: Head Fire grows to 95 acres in Napa County
headlines.description: A brush fire has spread to 95 acres in steep terrain near the Lake-Napa-Yolo county line, prompting Cal Fire to bring in air and ground crews to slow the fire’s spread, fire officials said Monday morning. The Head Fire was reported at 6:26 a.m. in the Berryessa Knoxville Road area in Napa County, near the borders of Lake and Yolo counties. By 10 a.m., the fire had grown to 95 acres with no containment, driven by winds and rugged terrain, according to Cal Fire. There was no immediate threat to nearby communities, and no evacuation orders have been issued, fire officials said. Cal Fire said they had dispatched 11 engines, seven water tenders, five hand crews, four dozers, one helicopter and multiple aircraft to battle the fire.

headlines.title: UC Berkeley to open Nancy Pelosi democracy institute
headlines.description: Former House Speaker Nancy Pelosi and UC Berkeley are launching a new, nonpartisan academic institute. The Nancy Pelosi Institute for Representative Democracy at UC Berkeley will be dedicated to strengthening American democracy. Launching in January 2027, the Pelosi Institute will develop and advance evidence-based solutions to democracy’s pressing problems. It will also create the next generation of civic leaders through teaching and research programs. Pelosi is leaving her longtime Bay Area congressional seat next year.

headlines.title: San Jose business collects relief supplies for Venezuela earthquake victims
headlines.description: Several businesses in the Bay Area are coming together to collect relief supplies for Venezuela following the deadly twin earthquakes that struck the nation. This includes a Venezuelan food truck in San Jose. The death toll in Venezuela is climbing as the desperate search for survivors continues following two major earthquakes. Authorities confirmed 1450 have died and nearly 69,000 are still missing. Several Bay area businesses came together to collect relief supplies, including Aquenono in San Jose. On Sunday, the husband and wife owners of the food truck business have been collecting everything, from gloves to food, to walkers and dog food. They say many of their clients have loved ones in Venezuela. Oakland 20 hours ago Venezuela Jun 28 San Jose Jun 28 “They can’t get in contact with family. There’s no news about their whereabouts of their family member and we hear many more stories like that from clients probably because their loved ones still have not been identified yet,” said Aquenono co-owner Melanie Portilla. Portilla says her brother-in-law lives in Venezuela and is grappling with aftershocks and power outages. So far, about 45 people have dropped off relief supplies, which will be brought to Oakland and later flown to Venezuela.

headlines.title: Volunteers start dismantling Pink Triangle on Twin Peaks in San Francisco
headlines.description: Volunteers started dismantling the Pink Triangle on Twin Peaks Sunday as San Francisco’s Pride festivities come to an end. For more than three decades now, the giant Pink Triangle on Twin Peaks has also been an expression of pride and resistance over San Francisco that can be seen from miles away during Pride Month. It took hundreds of volunteers more than three hours to tear down, and a lot longer than that to set up. Patrick Carney and a friend say they came up with this idea of a giant symbol of pride for their community more than 31 years ago over breakfast. They decided Twin Peaks was the perfect backdrop, so they painted huge canvases, sewed them together, and placed the first Pink Triangle here in the dark of night for everyone to see the next morning. 31 years later, the Pink Triangle has become this massive annual expression for the LGBTQ+ communiyt. Each side of the triangle is 240 feet long, and it takes 5,000 heavy-duty steel spikes to hold it in place in the Bay Area winds. Why the triangle? The triangle was used by the Nazi’s during World War II as a way to identify homosexuals in concentration camps. Carney says it stands as resistance to those ideas and as a symbol of hope and a lot more. “Resistance, courage, persistence, empowerment. But it started out during the holocaust, and then in the 70s it came back as a symbol of gay liberation and resistance”, he said. Volunteers stayed until 8 p.m. to take down the Pink Triangle and put it away in storage until Pride Month 2027 kicks off.

headlines.title: Volunteers needed in Oakland to help sort donations for Venezuela
headlines.description: A community in Oakland came together to collect money and donations for Venezuela following the deadly twin eartquakes that struck the country; now, they are asking for volunteers to help sort those donations. It has been four days since the deadly earthquakes that killed more than 1,400 in Venezuela. Workers at Oakland’s Arepas restaurant have been collecting money and donated supplies. The hundreds of boxes they have include food, medicine, clothing and diapers. However, organizers said that right now, the biggest need is for volunteers. Some volunteers are also needed to guard over the donations that have been arriving every day.

headlines.title: San Jose community mobilizes to support victims of Venezuela earthquakes
headlines.description: Watching the devastation in Venezuela has prompted dozens of people throughout the Bay Area to spring into action to donate relief supplies. On Saturday, there was a donation drive at Arepas Latin Cuisine, a Venezuelan restaurant with locations in San Francisco and San Jose; many who went to the San Jose location to donate said they have relatives in Venezuela, and they are heartbroken the death toll is rising. Dorly Lopez’s cousins live in Venezuela, so for her, the mission is personal. “They have to move. And some fear there is going to be a tsunami,” she said. Lopez stopped at the restaurant with full arms and a heavy heart. Venezuela Jun 25 Venezuela Jun 27 Venezuela Jun 25 “We want to help. we see the disaster in Venezuela we are heartbroken,” she said. Lopez was among many others who were dropping off sleeping pads, clothing, water, first aid kits and diapers at the restaurant. There were so many donations that employees had to stuff the items into a closet and a van. Not only is Arepas collecting supplies, but the restaurant is also donating a portion of the profits from meals served here to relief efforts in Venezuela. For Marianny Mata, watching the devastation from the quake has been especially painful. “I was born and raised in Venezuela and my family lives there,” she said. Mata worries about those close to her. “My godmother… her uncle is missing. He’s been missing and they still haven’t found him,” she said. A video from Caracas captured the moment buildings collapsed following two back-to-back magnitude seven plus quakes, prompting the Arepas restaurant in San Francisco to also collect supplies for families in need. “It made me feel horrible made me feel responsible. I just really need to do something,” restaurant owner Joanna Torres said. As the frantic search for survivors continues in Venezuela, more than four thousand miles away people in the Bay Area are answering the call to help. Medical, baby supplies and nonperishable food were top priorities. The first shipment of donations was expected to head to the Red Cross in Caracas on Tuesday.

headlines.title: Firefighters battle large fire at a home in Castro Valley
headlines.description: Firefighters were battling a large fire at a home in Castro Valley. The fire was reported just before 4:15 p.m. at a home on Queen Street. According to authorities, the flames also threatened other homes nearby, prompting a three-alarm response. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates.
